clinical_indications: Severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis of any volume leading to drop in hemoglobin (> 1g/dL)
Severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis of any volume leading to drop in hematocrit (> 5%)
Severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis of any volume leading to respiratory failure (SPaO2 < 60%)
Severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis of any volume leading to hypotension (systolic BP < 90 mm Hg)
First-line treatment for surgically unfit patients
First-line treatment for patients with diffuse or bilateral lung disease

clinical_thresholds.note: for respiratory failure in severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis

preauth.label: Clinical notes with planned line of treatment

claim.label: Check angiography of same bronchial artery after procedure

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure submitted?
Was the chest X ray of patient submitted?
Does the HRCT chest/ CTPA of patient suggestive of bronchial artery hemorrhage?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed Procedure / Operative notes submitted?
Does the post procedure angiogram of affected bronchial artery show thrombosis of the artery?
Is there a Detailed Discharge Summary submitted?

common_queries: Clinical notes do not confirm the patient suffering from hemoptysis.
HRCT Chest/CTPA not submitted or does not suggest bronchial artery hemorrhage.
Post-procedure angiogram not submitted or does not show thrombosis of the affected bronchial artery.
Clinical notes are missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure.

clinical_key_pointers: Hemoptysis is an important clinical and potentially grave condition with diverse inflammatory, neoplastic, and vascular etiologies.
Precise anatomic localization of hemorrhage poses a challenge for clinicians.
Bronchial artery angiography with embolization (BAE) has become a mainstay in treatment.
Moderate hemoptysis: 100–300 mL/day or > 3 bouts of > 100 mL/day in one week.

Severe/massive/life-threatening hemoptysis: > 300 mL/day, or any volume leading to drop in hemoglobin (> 1g/dL) or hematocrit (> 5%), or respiratory failure (SPaO2 < 60%), or hypotension (systolic BP < 90 mm Hg).
Worldwide, the most common cause of hemoptysis is active tuberculosis.
Other etiologies: aspergillosis, cystic fibrosis, bronchiectasis, bronchogenic carcinoma, congenital heart disease, cryptogenic.

Diagnostic evaluation includes: thorough history and physical examination, signs/symptoms of infection/vasculitis/granulomatous/airway disease, chest radiography, bronchoscopy, and CTA (for cause detection and vascular roadmap).
Historically, surgery was definitive but carries high mortality (~18% elective, 40% emergent).
Conservative management with medication has high mortality (~50%).
BAE is a minimally invasive procedure, potentially offered as first-line treatment or a bridge to more definitive intervention.
Overall immediate clinical success rate of BAE (complete cessation of hemoptysis) varies from 70%-99%.
Recurrence rate remains high (10%–57%) due to incomplete initial embolization, recanalization, and recruitment of new collaterals.
BAE continues to be the first-line, minimally invasive treatment of hemoptysis in emergency settings, surgically unfit patients, or in patients with diffuse or bilateral lung disease.
surgical_options: Historically, surgery has been the definitive therapy for hemoptysis, with mortality ~18% for elective procedures and up to 40% for emergent ones.
